one_line_takeaway: SBU is the first framework to jointly unlearn from both an LLM agent's parameters (via KL-divergence alignment toward a random reference model) and its external memory (via dependency-closure graph pruning with reference counting), showing on medical-QA benchmarks that synchronizing both pathways substantially raises MIA-based privacy scores (e.g., 0.7267 to 0.8953 on MedQA QF=100) while better preserving test/generalization accuracy than parameter-only baselines like NPO and GA, which either fail to suppress membership signals or catastrophically damage utility.
key_finding: On MedQA (QF=100), SBU reduces forget accuracy to 73.00% while raising MIA Score to 0.8953 -- described in the paper as a 24.8% privacy improvement -- versus Sequential LoRA's MIA Score of 0.7167 (forget 89.33%, test 88.67%) and the unmodified Origin model's 0.7267 (forget 89.33%, test 91.00%); SBU's test/gen accuracy (92.50%/90.50%) exceeds all baselines shown in Table 2. On MedMCQA (QF=1000, Table 3), SBU reaches MIA Score 0.9960 with test/gen 89.33%/89.00%, versus baselines clustered around MIA Score 0.90-0.98. NPO shows the lowest raw forget accuracy on MedQA QF=100 (74.00% per the text; 90.00% reported in Table 2's NPO row, an apparent inconsistency between prose and table) but exhibits 'catastrophic over-unlearning,' collapsing generalization to 41.67% (MedQA) and 62.33% (MedReason QF=1000). The memory pathway alone (Table 4) drops MedQA memory-level forget accuracy from 78.0% to 14.0% while retain accuracy on memory rises 54.0% to 56.0%; similar reductions hold for MedMCQA (84.0%->30.0% forget) and MedReason (68.0%->26.0% forget). The ablation (Table 6) shows 'w/o Mem' (parameter-only) achieves MIA Score 0.834 vs. full SBU's 0.895, and 'Mem-Only' achieves only 0.758, evidencing that both pathways are needed and are complementary rather than redundant. Memory-side ablation (Table 5) shows Naive Deletion leaves MIA AUC at 0.5467 (weakest), Re-indexing improves it to 0.5180, and SBU's dependency-aware deletion (MIA AUC 0.5000) matches or slightly beats the Retraining Oracle (0.5020) without full memory reconstruction. Hyperparameter sweep on MedMCQA: optimal lambda_F=1.5, T=2.0 with entropy fallback gives 90% test accuracy and 68% forget accuracy; disabling entropy fallback under identical settings drops test accuracy from 90% to 78% and raises forget accuracy from 68% to 80%; lambda_F below 0.5 under-forgets (86% forget accuracy) while lambda_F >= 3.0 over-forgets and damages utility (66% test accuracy, 64% forget accuracy); high temperature (T=4.0) destabilizes training, dropping test accuracy to 68%. In the 6-turn Agent Loop case study, the forget-set retrieval hit rate drops from 100% to 0% immediately after the T4 delete request while the retain-set hit rate stays stable, and derived-summary cleanup tracks the dependency-closure removal.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (per its stated contributions list and Method section) is: (1) defining and formalizing 'agentic unlearning' as the problem of removing information from an LLM agent that holds knowledge both in model parameters (theta) and in an evolving external memory M, and identifying 'parameter-memory backflow' -- a closed-loop recontamination phenomenon where retrieval reactivates parametric remnants or memory-derived artifacts reintroduce deleted content -- as the core obstacle that renders parameter-only unlearning insufficient for memory-augmented agents; and (2) Synchronized Backflow Unlearning (SBU), a dual-pathway framework with (a) a memory pathway that models memory as a dependency graph G=(V,E) over raw episodic memories M, semantic summaries S, reflections R, and knowledge-graph nodes K, using instant blocklisting (O(1) hash-set membership checks), dependency-closure computation Dep(DF) to identify derived artifacts uniquely dependent on forgotten data, reference-count decrementing to preserve shared artifacts, and periodic vector-index rebuilding when the blocklist exceeds threshold tau=100; and (b) a parameter pathway ('Entropy-Regularized Parameter Unlearning' / 'KL-to-random') that trains the model with a combined loss L_weight = L_CE^DR + lambda_F * T^2 * L_KL^DF, i.e. standard cross-entropy on the retain set plus a KL-divergence term pulling the model's output distribution on the forget set toward that of a frozen, randomly initialized reference model, aiming for high-entropy (maximally uncertain) rather than confidently-wrong outputs on forget queries. The two pathways are executed in a fixed sequence per round (memory pathway first, then parameter pathway) so parameter optimization runs on an already-sanitized retrieval context, and all deletion operations are logged in a tamper-evident, hash-chained write-ahead log for auditability. This is presented as the first formal treatment of agentic (memory+parameter) unlearning for memory-augmented LLM agents.
threat_model: The paper's privacy threat is unauthorized retention/re-exposure of sensitive information (e.g., patient data in medical QA agents) after a deletion request, via two channels that can reinforce each other: (i) retrieval-based backflow -- content nominally deleted from persistent external memory can still be regenerated by the model's parametric knowledge and then written back into memory by the agent's normal write loop, and (ii) memory-based re-exposure -- derived artifacts (summaries, reflections, knowledge-graph entities) built from forgotten raw memories can persist and leak the deleted content even if the raw entry is removed, and retrieval prompts can reactivate parametric traces the model was never made to forget. The attacker/evaluator capability modeled for verification is a membership inference attack (MIA): the adversary observes loss distributions over member (should-be-forgotten) vs. non-member data and tries to distinguish them; the paper reports an AUC-based MIA metric (A near 0.5 = ideal unlearning, near 1 = under-unlearning/still detectable membership, near 0 = over-unlearning). This is a privacy/data-removal threat model (regulatory framing: HIPAA/GDPR compliance for medical agents), not an adversarial-prompt-injection or jailbreak threat model; the paper does not model an active adversary crafting queries to extract secrets beyond the standard MIA loss-based test.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitation (Conclusion) is that dependency tracking may not fully capture cross-agent information flow in shared knowledge graphs, motivating future work on multi-agent unlearning protocols. Additional limitations evident from the paper's own text but not explicitly flagged as limitations by the authors: (1) evaluation is confined to a single base model (II-Medical-8B/Qwen3-8B) and a single domain (medical QA), so generalization to other agent architectures, memory implementations, or domains is untested; (2) the MIA-based privacy metric evaluates membership inference over loss distributions but the paper does not test against stronger/adaptive extraction attacks (e.g., prompt-based data extraction, jailbreak-style probing) beyond the standard MIA test; (3) the 'unified implementation details,' cryptographic verification scheme, and audit-log construction are deferred to unreferenced 'supplementary material' not present in the extracted main text, so the tamper-evident logging claim cannot be independently verified from the main paper; (4) there is an apparent numerical inconsistency between the prose ('NPO attains the lowest forget (74.00%)') and Table 2 (NPO forget listed as 90.00±5.29%), which the review flags as a grounding caveat rather than resolving; (5) the ethical statement notes the datasets contain no real patient identifiers, so the medical-privacy motivation is validated on synthetic/exam-style QA data rather than real protected health information, and the authors note clinical deployment would require additional regulatory review.

datasets_used: MedMCQA (~183k multiple-choice questions, 2,400 topics, 21 medical subjects; Pal et al. 2022)
MedQA (~10k professional board-exam multiple-choice questions; Jin et al. 2021)
MedReason (~33k QA pairs, reasoning annotations excluded; Wu et al. 2025)
quantitative_results: MedQA memory-level forget accuracy drops from 78.0% to 14.0% after memory unlearning; retain accuracy rises 54.0% to 56.0% (Table 4)

Ablation (Table 6, MedQA QF=100): w/o Mem MIA Score 0.834 (forget 74.33, test 91.00, gen 89.00); Mem-Only MIA Score 0.758 (forget 85.67, test 88.33, gen 86.67); Ours MIA Score 0.895 (forget 73.00, test 92.50, gen 90.50)
Memory-side ablation (Table 5, MedMCQA QF=100): Naive Deletion MIA AUC 0.5467, Re-indexing 0.5180, Retraining Oracle 0.5020, Ours 0.5000

Hyperparameter sweep (MedMCQA): optimal lambda_F=1.5, T=2.0 with entropy fallback -> 90% test acc, 68% forget acc; without entropy fallback, same settings -> 78% test acc, 80% forget acc; lambda_F>=3.0 -> 66% test acc, 64% forget acc; T=4.0 -> 68% test acc
Baseline model II-Medical-8B accuracy with vs. without memory augmentation (Table 1): MedQA 78.39%->88.00%, MedMCQA 84.71%->87.67%, MedReason 70.00%->87.67%, Avg 77.70%->87.78%
baselines_compared: Retraining Oracle (memory-only, reconstructs memory from retain set)
